metadata.title: Small town propaganda: The content and emotions of politicized digital local news in the United States

abstract: Increasing numbers of websites in the United States spread politically conservative propaganda while masquerading as legitimate digital local news outlets. How are these sites attempting to broadcast their ideology and shape readers' understanding? In this research note, we analyze an original dataset comprising 122,054 articles from propagandistic websites and 90,689 articles from the websites of corresponding institutional local news organizations. The results suggest that the doppelgänger sites use three strategies: copy locally relevant text available online, such as summaries of gasoline prices and press releases; seize on national political controversies; and deploy emotions in ways that can entice moderate readers to further engage the sites. In addition, a supplemental analysis indicates that conservative propagandists also embed subtle, implicit probusiness rhetoric into mundane articles about state and local economic policy. We conclude by highlighting directions for further research on locally tailored digital conservative propaganda.

sections.paper_id: Karell and Agrawal - 2022 - Small town propaganda The content and emotions of politicized digital local news in the United Stat
paragraphs: Partisan propaganda has gone local. Recent investigative journalism has identified increasing numbers of politicized websites in the United States that masquerade as small-and medium-sized towns' homegrown news outlets (e.g., Bengani, 2019;Levin, 2019;Nyhan, 2019). Similar to political native advertising (Dai & Luqiu, 2020), except on the scale of an entire publication and website, these sites visually mimic the websites of established local news organizations, present copy written by conservative groups as "regular" news stories, and intermingle ideological content with truthful local announcements and reports (see Fig. 1 for an example 1 ). In other words, the sites are not "fake" or completely untruthful; their visual and textual content are sometimes accurate. 2 Instead, it is more useful to think of the websites as disinformation, although, as we explain below, we prefer to conceptualize them as conservative digital propaganda.

In this research note, we present a preliminary analysis of the propagandistic sites' text to begin uncovering how they attempt to spread their messaging and shape readers' understanding. A large (and quickly growing) body of research has examined the text of "fake news", mis/disinformation, and untruthful documents. Much of this research focuses on distinguishing the so-called fake news from "true" information by developing models that, for example, use a variety of grammatical and stylistic text-based features (e.g., Papadopoulou, Zampoglou, Papadopoulos & Kompatsiaris, 2017), supplement text measurement with network and behavioral features (e.g., Conroy, Rubin & Chen, 2015), and combine techniques from computational linguistics and deep neural network approaches (e.g., Ghosh & Shah, 2019). We build on some of this "detection" literature, but our aim differs slightly. Rather than develop identification techniques-we already know which news sites are propaganda, aftet all-our analysis measures and compares specific features of the locally tailored conservative digital propaganda that could facilitate its broadcasting of ideology. By doing so, we hope to, first, help expand the scope of the scholarship on right-wing disinformation and propaganda, which frequently adopts national-level and cross-national designs (e.g., Benkler, Faris & Roberts, 2018;Bennett & Livingston, 2018;Hameleers, 2020;Hameleers & Minihold, 2020;Nielsen & Graves, 2017), and, second, lay a foundation for further research on how conservative digital propaganda is affecting local communities across the United States and other liberal democracies.

As with other forms of propaganda, shaping readers' understanding through digital local news sites requires accomplishing at least two tasks: wrapping the propagandists' ideology or ideas in seemingly innocuous subject matter and encouraging audience members to read this messaging. Therefore, in our analysis, we compare two features across propagandistic and established local news websites. First, articles' subject matter, or content, which can be used to both convey and mask the propagandists' (and publishers') political ideals. Second, the use of emotion, which can bolster engagement (e.g., Bakir & McStay, 2018;van der Meer & Verhoeven, 2014). In addition, we measure changes in the content and emotion from 2017 through 2019, which can offer evidence of locally focused conservative propagandists adapting their strategies. Since we do not examine all the techniques propagandistic sites could use for manipulation, such as the design and selection of images or use of metaphor and hyperbole, we make our data available for further research. 3

sections.paper_id: Karell and Agrawal - 2022 - Small town propaganda The content and emotions of politicized digital local news in the United Stat
paragraphs: The politicized local news websites we study would strike many as "fake news". Yet, since the notion of "fake news" now means different things to different people (Nielsen & Graves, 2017) and has become a normative concept (Hameleers, 2020;Hameleers & Minihold, 2020), our "fake news" material is more usefully conceptualized as politicized digital disinformation. It is dishonest and deliberately deceptive online communication with a political aim (Bennett & Livingston, 2018;Hameleers, 2020;Hameleers & Minihold, 2020;Marwick & Lewis, 2017). However, we prefer to understand this material as propaganda, as defined by Bakir, Herring, Millar and Robinson (2019). Labeling material disinformation-and in some cases, also propaganda (e.g., Benkler et al., 2018)-often requires assumptions about the creators' intent, for example, that they were deliberate and purposefully seeking to manipulate audience members. In contrast, Bakir et al. (2019) offer a definition of propaganda that places greater emphasis on the substance of the material, remaining agnostic about creators' intent. For Bakir and colleagues, propaganda is communication that is non-consensual-it does not provide readers, viewers, or listeners the information they need to make rational and informed decisions based on the communication. Of course, this view of propaganda overlaps with definitions of mis/disinformation-it can be deceptive, for example-and it challenges or omits aspects of other definitions of propaganda (e.g., Ellul, 1973;Herman & Chomsky, 1998;Bernays, 2005;Benkler et al., 2018;Zollmann, 2019). Nevertheless, it is useful for our purposes here: it is a clear and parsimonious way to understand what our data are. Therefore, we refer to the articles from the conservative doppelgänger websites as "propaganda" and "propagandistic".

We compare the propaganda to articles published on websites run by established local news organizations. Of course, these comparison articles could also be considered a type of propaganda. Ideological slants are common in "real" news media (Della Vigna & Kaplan, 2007;Martin & McCrain, 2019) and mass media in liberal democracies often bolster hegemonic ideas and centers of power (Herman & Chomsky, 1998;Lazerfeld & Merton [1948], 1957;Zollmann, 2019). In other words, "regular", "real", or established mass media often represent institutionalized interests. For this reason, we refer to articles published on the websites of localities' familiar and long-existing local news organizations as "institutional".

Today, propagandists exploit the characteristics of digital and social media to shape the perceptions of their audience, such as by using (semi-)automated bots to create the sense that a particular viewpoint is widespread (Woolley & Howard, 2019), linking social media accounts to move extreme ideas into the mainstream (Benkler et al., 2018), and, our focus here, create mock representations of familiar and trusted sources of information (Bengani, 2019). Yet, despite the novelty of these tactics, contemporary digital propaganda campaigns use common and well-recognized methods. At a fundamental level, they evoke the efforts depicted in (Bernays's [1928], 2005) seminal study of propaganda, which highlighted two basic strategies underlying how propaganda operates: it contains subject matter, or content, which both obscures and conveys the propogandists' message; second, it gives people a reason to pay attention to the content. At a more refined level, digital propagandists have honed these strategies, adopting techniques described in recent research, such as the use of emotion and an entertaining (hedonic) style (e.g., Araujo, 2019;Bakir & McStay, 2018;van der Meer & Verhoeven, 2014). Thus, we organize our analysis around two basic strategies from Bernays ([1928] 2005)-the content and enticement of readers to engage the content-and, regarding the latter, focus on one element of enticement identified by contemporary scholarship, the deployment of emotion.

In our case of local news websites, the content will generally be about local and regional matters. Yet, perhaps some particular topics are more prevalent than others, and this distribution may differ between the two media types. Therefore, the first part of our analysis examines differences in content both across media types and within types over time (2017 through 2019). Any differences we detect could indicate how propogandists think they can best convey their ideas to their readers.

Enticing readers can be accomplished in various ways. We examine the use of emotion, and particularly the emotions of anger, disgust, and anticipation. We select these emotions because the propaganda we collected is all politically conservative, 4,5 which is the result of what investigative journalism has uncovered (see Bengani, 2019;Levin, 2019), as well as the fact that while both right-and 3 We purposefully do not measure ideology. We already know that the propagandistic sites are ideologically right-wing, so our goal is to show how this ideology can be concurrently communicated and hidden within sites-an obfuscation that facilitates the manipulation at the core of propaganda. Future research can use our data to analyze ideological signatures. 4 By "politically conservative", we mean that the messaging, ideology, and ownership of the propaganda websites align with the institutional, mainstream portion of the political right in the United States (see Bengani 2019;Levin 2019). In contrast, the sites' message, ideology, and ownership do not fit with other portions of the political right, such as: the racist-or anti-immigrant wings (which largely have a singular focus on immigration and racial belonging); the protest wing (which often rejects the political system; the propaganda sites seemingly want to manipulate the system to achieve their goals); and the extreme or unreconciled wing, which adopt a revolutionary perspective and advocate an alternative to the democratic system (Fennema 1997;Fawcett, 2020). 5 The websites could be further considered as part of the "politically conservative 'alt-right'", if we adopt Bennet and Livingston's (2018) definition of the alt-right as a collection of online "sites and platforms that produce and distribute disinformation in order to advance partisan agendas and to destabilize opponents and institutions" (pg. 125). However, others reserve the term "alt-right" for media that espouse extreme messaging and ideology (e.g., Faris, et al. 2017). For the purposes of this research note, it is sufficient to understand the propaganda as: (1) digital;
(2) sharing many attributes with common conceptualizations of disinformation; and (3) evocative of the ideology and goals of mainstream, conservative politicians and groups in the United States.

left-leaning groups use disinformation and propaganda, groups on the political right in the United States and other liberal democracies are more active in creating, spreading, and discussing disinformation, "fake news", and partisan propaganda (e.g., Benkler et al., 2018;Bennett & Livingston, 2018;Hameleers, 2020;Hameleers & Minihold, 2020). The political slant of our propaganda matters since, in the United States, particular emotions have come to play central roles in conservative politics. For instance, anger and disgust help power the formation and maintenance of the political right: anger over the loss of status has driven so-called mainstream Americans towards the conservative party (McVeigh & Estep, 2019); feelings of betrayal have sparked the formation of new right-wing groups (Belew, 2018); and distaste for out-group members tends to strengthen individuals' attachment to such groups (Simi, Blee, DeMichele & Windisch, 2017).

The third emotion, anticipation, or a sense of expectation and discovery, can be useful to digital news sites for drawing in politically moderate readers from a general readership, especially if it is deployed at particular points of articles, such as the beginning or end. While propagandists' use of some emotions, such as anger, can often cause moderates to react with feelings of shock and exasperation (Jasper, 2011), the emotion of anticipation can potentially attract readers by making them feel part of an exciting and engaging truth-uncovering endeavor (Durnova, 2019; see also Araujo, 2019). In addition, the use of anticipation does not overtly expose sites' propagandistic nature to readers-it inspires a sense of discovery, not anger at a particular target-thereby minimizing the risk of a backlash.

In contrast, the sites' message, ideology, and ownership do not fit with other portions of the political right, such as: the racist-or anti-immigrant wings (which largely have a singular focus on immigration and racial belonging); the protest wing (which often rejects the political system; the propaganda sites seemingly want to manipulate the system to achieve their goals); and the extreme or unreconciled wing, which adopt a revolutionary perspective and advocate an alternative to the democratic system (Fennema 1997;Fawcett, 2020). 5 The websites could be further considered as part of the "politically conservative 'alt-right'", if we adopt Bennet and Livingston's (2018) definition of the alt-right as a collection of online "sites and platforms that produce and distribute disinformation in order to advance partisan agendas and to destabilize opponents and institutions" (pg. 125). However, others reserve the term "alt-right" for media that espouse extreme messaging and ideology (e.g., Faris, et al. 2017). For the purposes of this research note, it is sufficient to understand the propaganda as: (1) digital;

(2) sharing many attributes with common conceptualizations of disinformation; and (3) evocative of the ideology and goals of mainstream, conservative politicians and groups in the United States.

left-leaning groups use disinformation and propaganda, groups on the political right in the United States and other liberal democracies are more active in creating, spreading, and discussing disinformation, "fake news", and partisan propaganda (e.g., Benkler et al., 2018;Bennett & Livingston, 2018;Hameleers, 2020;Hameleers & Minihold, 2020). The political slant of our propaganda matters since, in the United States, particular emotions have come to play central roles in conservative politics. For instance, anger and disgust help power the formation and maintenance of the political right: anger over the loss of status has driven so-called mainstream Americans towards the conservative party (McVeigh & Estep, 2019); feelings of betrayal have sparked the formation of new right-wing groups (Belew, 2018); and distaste for out-group members tends to strengthen individuals' attachment to such groups (Simi, Blee, DeMichele & Windisch, 2017).

The third emotion, anticipation, or a sense of expectation and discovery, can be useful to digital news sites for drawing in politically moderate readers from a general readership, especially if it is deployed at particular points of articles, such as the beginning or end. While propagandists' use of some emotions, such as anger, can often cause moderates to react with feelings of shock and exasperation (Jasper, 2011), the emotion of anticipation can potentially attract readers by making them feel part of an exciting and engaging truth-uncovering endeavor (Durnova, 2019; see also Araujo, 2019). In addition, the use of anticipation does not overtly expose sites' propagandistic nature to readers-it inspires a sense of discovery, not anger at a particular target-thereby minimizing the risk of a backlash.
sections.preview_head: The politicized local news websites we study would strike many as "fake news". Yet, since the notion of "fake news" now means different things to different people (Nielsen & Graves, 2017) and has become a normative concept (Hameleers, 2020;Hameleers & Minihold, 2020), our "fake news" material is more usefully conceptualized as politicized digital disinformation. It is dishonest and deliberately deceptive online communication with a political aim (Bennett & Livingston, 2018;Hameleers, 2020;Hameleers & Minihold, 2020;Marwick & Lewis, 2017).

sections.paper_id: Karell and Agrawal - 2022 - Small town propaganda The content and emotions of politicized digital local news in the United Stat
paragraphs: To enable future wide-ranging analyses of propagandistic and institutional local news websites, such as an analysis of trends in local media news sites before the rise of the competing online propaganda, we first created a large database of articles from both types of sites. This database covers the years from 2003, the year of the earliest articles we could find, through 2017, the year of the first appearances of the propagandistic local news sites in the United States, and up to early 2020, when we ended our data collection efforts.

We began building the database by visiting the propagandistic sites listed in two prominent reports (i.e., Bengani, 2019;Levin, 2019). These reports identified the sites as partisan propaganda-and what Bengani calls "pink slime journalism"-based not only on reading some of the sites' content, but also on which individuals and organizations owned and funded the sites, a strategy often used in related scholarly research (e.g., Qin, Strömberg & Wu, 2018;Stockmann, 2013). For each site on the list, we used a custom web scraper to collect all their articles' text and metadata, such as date of publication, URLs of embedded images, and section (i.e., Business, Sports). Whenever possible, we also collected the main image associated with the articles. Next, we visited "linked" sites. Many of the propagandistic sites linked to other websites in their "news network", which were typically similar looking sites owned by the same entity yet tailored for a different locality (Bengani, 2019). We also collected these linked sites' article text, metadata, and images, then continued chain sampling and gathering data until we could not find new propagandistic local news sites. We ended with 147,948 articles from 381 propagandistic sites dating from January 23, 2017 to March 6, 2020.

Then, for each locality with a propagandistic local news site, we visited the website of its institutional local newspaper. From these "real" sites, we used our scraper to collect a random sample of online articles-text and metadata-from each section that appeared in the corresponding propagandistic news site. For example, if a locality's propagandistic news site had the sections "News", "Business", and "Sports", we collected articles from only these sections in the corresponding institutional site.

As a result of our data collection procedures, the selected propagandistic and institutional outlets match geographically, and their articles are drawn from the same newspaper sections. The geographic match is not always exact, however. When a locality had a propagandistic site but no institutional local news site or when the locality was unclear (e.g., "Northern Arizona"), we collected data from the website of the newspaper based in the capital city of the locality's state. 6 As a result, the database contains "real" articles from 11 unique locations rather than the 37 unique localities associated with the propagandistic sites. We gathered 353,261 articles from 11 institutional sites dating from January 4, 2003 to January 19, 2020. The complete database of articles-both propagandistic and institutional-contains 501,209 articles from 392 local news sites representing 48 unique localities. We make this database publicly available with this research note.

Since our analysis is a comparison of propagandistic and institutional sites, we next created an analytical dataset by selecting all the articles from the main database that were published in the years during which propagandistic and institutional sites overlapped. This analytical dataset contains articles from the start of 2017, the first year propagandistic sites were online, through 2019, the final complete year of data collection. The analytical dataset comprises 212,743 articles: 122,054 articles from 380 propagandistic sites and 90,689 articles from four established sites (Table 1). 7 We use the analytical dataset to examine how articles' content differs (or not) across the two types of media. Then, to analyze how emotions are deployed throughout articles, we reconfigure this dataset in two ways. First, we parse each article into sentences, which 6 If there was more than one institutional local newspaper website for the selected location, such as a state capital, we used the website (of an institutional local news organization) that appeared first in a list of Google search results. 7 The analytical dataset contains 122,054 propagandistic articles instead of the 147,948 articles in the complete database because of two reasons.

First, articles published in 2020 (January through March) were removed. Second, a few articles were removed during the topic modeling analysis, explained below, because, for example, they were very short in length and/or consisted only of rare words.

gives us the ability to track changes within articles. Second, we removed articles with fewer than nine sentences. This is an initial attempt at ensuring that the articles in the analysis have a meaningful beginning, middle, and end-in this case, about three sentences each. 8 Future research can explore our data to determine other ways for identifying articles that allow for emotions to vary within the articles' text.

The two reconfigurations produced a distinct sentence-level dataset used for the analysis of emotions. Table 2 summarizes this dataset. We see that it contains fewer propaganda articles than the main analytical dataset. This is because many of the propaganda articles are relatively short (see the number of sentences per article reported in Table 1), and therefore were not retained when we sampled articles with nine or more sentences. Removing these short propaganda articles has the benefit of making the number of sentences per article more similar across the two media type samples (compare the median sentences per article in Tables 1 and 2).

Data collection occurred during April and May 2020. We also make both the article-level and sentence-level dataset available with this research note. The former contains the articles' content and metadata, as well as the values of several measures, including topics, emotional prevalence, and many stylometrics. 9

sections.paper_id: Karell and Agrawal - 2022 - Small town propaganda The content and emotions of politicized digital local news in the United Stat
paragraphs: Our analysis examines two aspects of the material published on propagandistic and institutional sites: the content of articles and the emotions expressed in articles. We measured the former using a structural topic model (STM). Structural topic models are unsupervised machine learning models that represent documents within a corpus as a mixture of latent topics. They identify the topics by iteratively sampling from the corpus and using the co-occurrence of words in documents to estimate the set of topics best representing patterns of word usage in the corpus. They additionally model how each document is optimally represented by a configuration of the topics. 10 Structural topic models are especially helpful for comparing content between two types of documents, such as articles from either propagandistic or institutional websites. Specifically, STMs estimate how the prevalence of topics and their word-level composition depend on document attributes, captured in the metadata (Roberts et al., 2014) These estimates illuminate both the topics embedded in the documents of a corpus and the prevalence of these topics across the document types (Parthasarathy, Rao & Palaniswamy, 2019). Moreover, other information about the documents can be included in the model as covariates, which help to identify the topics while conditioning for systematic differences not of primary interest (Lucas et al., 2015). In our case, the results were obtained from a model that included variables for articles' type of site and year of publication, as well as the United States state their website was associated with. Estimating STMs requires processing the text data and selecting the number of topics to model; we explain these in Appendix B of the Supplementary Materials.

Next, we examined the emotion conveyed in articles by applying a dictionary-based measurement to our sentence-level dataset. Dictionary-based methods draw on a pre-defined collection of words, or a dictionary, that have been assigned particular connotations, such as a sentiment or emotion. The method counts the number of in-dictionary words in each document, then typically sums these words (and normalizes the sum) to discern a document-level attribute, such as a prevalent emotion.

While there are limitations to using dictionary techniques for measurement, they have the advantages of transparency and interpretability. In addition, a general social science readership is likely to be most familiar with dictionary approaches since they have been used in several studies published in high-profile, generalist journals (e.g., Bonikowski & Gidron, 2016;Paxton, Velasco & Ressler, 2020). Moreover, we see our analysis as a first step in a broader research program on emotions in "post-truth" politics and media (see Durnova, 2019). As part of the initial stage of this program, we believe there is value is evaluating the results of simple, straightforward methods before using more complex methods that are difficult to understand and interpret. Appendix C of the Supplementary Materials provides further discussion and details of the emotion measurement technique and analysis.

Karell and Agrawal - 2022 - Small town propaganda The content and emotions of politicized digital local news in the United Stat
paragraphs: Our analysis indicates that the propagandistic and insititutional local news sites share 10 topics to varying degrees: business friendly profiles; children, students, and education; gasoline prices; meal recipes; the Mueller investigation into Russia's interference with the 2016 United States presidential election; official economic indicators; police reports; sports; state welfare policies; and Veterans Affairs (VA) hospital reports. (See Appendix B of the Supplementary Materials for an explanation of how we identified the 10 topic solution and Appendix D for a detailed interpretation of the topics.) Fig. 2 shows the estimated prevalence of topics across media types over time. We first see that no single topic dominates either kind of site. All topics' estimated proportions are below 0.5, and all but one are below 0.25. We also see that the prevalence of some content differs significantly across media types, using conventional levels of statistical significance. The topics that appear more in propaganda 8 We additionally removed sentences shorter than two words. When sentences shorter than three words (rather than two) were removed, the results were consistent with the reported findings. 9 We do not discuss the stylometrics here; we can provide further information upon request. In brief, we calculated two dozen common stylometrics using packages available for use with Python and R. 10 For readers interested in more details about topic models, there are many introductory articles, books, and online resources. We recommend
Mohr & Bogandov 2013, in this journal, as well as Grimmer & Stewart 2013.
D. Karell and A. Agrawal Poetics 92 (2022) 101641

articles are about gasoline prices, the Mueller investigation, official economic indicators (but only in 2018 and 2019), and state welfare policies. However, for these and the other topics, the estimated differences in prevalence across media types are small, usually not more than 10% of sites' content. In addition, there is little change over time. The topic capturing official economic indicators in propagandistic sites has the greatest rate of growth over time, but, again, its overall prevalence remains low.

We now focus on the estimated prevalence of four topics; these specific results provide important insight into how propagandistic sites use articles' subject matter to convey ideology. Two of the most widespread topics on propagandistic sites are about gasoline prices and official economic indicators. The articles consisting mostly of these topics are nearly all copied verbatim from text found Fig. 2. The estimated proportions of topics across media types over time. The 95% confidence intervals are depicted as shaded bands, but most are small and obscured by the plotted lines. Lines do not appear when the expected proportion equals zero.

elsewhere online. That is, propagandistic sites are distinguished in part by the practice of gathering localized information readily and freely found online, such as published gasoline prices, bureaucratic reports made public, and official press releases. This kind of subject matter fills their pages with truthful locally relevant information, helping to hide ideological messaging that is present in other articles. 11 What are these other articles about? A third topic that is more prevalent on the propagandistic sites offers a partial answer: political controversy, such as the Mueller investigation of Russia's interference into the 2016 United States presidential election. sections.paper_id: Karell and Agrawal - 2022 - Small town propaganda The content and emotions of politicized digital local news in the United Stat
paragraphs: While the content analysis explores how propagandistic sites convey and hide ideology, our analysis of emotion sheds light on how the sites might entice individuals to read the content. Fig. 3 shows the average rolling median of emotional prevalence in sentences through media types' articles across years. The rolling median is calculated throughout the extent of articles when articles are normalized by length, with the left-hand end of each panel represents the beginning of articles and the right-hand side represents the end. The figure also shows one standard deviation from the mean of the articles' median sentence-level number of emotion words. 12 We see, first, that the propaganda and institutional articles use anticipation more than anger and disgust, and they do so at similar levels. Second, the overall levels of anger and disgust are also roughly similar in propaganda and institutional articles, although the upper bound of the standard deviation for propaganda articles is consistently higher. This indicates that while the propaganda and institutional sites mostly have articles with comparable levels of anger and disgust, the former sites have more content with a higher prevalence of anger and disgust.

We now discuss the prevalence of emotions within articles. Specifically, are articles saturated with emotion or do they have an emotional rhythm, with peaks and troughs of emotion at strategic points? Fig. 3 shows that the usage of anger and disgust within propaganda articles mostly parallels their usage in institutional articles. Their prevalence decreases slightly at the end of articles, regardless of media type and year of publication. These findings suggest that propaganda sites at times offer readers angrier and more disapproving versions of the emotional rhythms found in institutional outlets. This pattern aligns with previous research emphasizing the importance of anger and disgust for mobilizing and sustaining right-wing groups in the United States (e.g., Belew, 2018, Simi et al., 2017).

In contrast, the use of anticipation in some propagandistic articles deviates from the overall pattern in institutional articles at the end of the articles. We see in Fig. 3 that, mostly at the end of articles, the upper bound of the standard deviation for propaganda articles surpasses that of the institutional articles. This happens in each year, but most noticeably in 2017 and 2018. We posit that this greater usage of the anticipation emotion at the end of an article may engender in readers a sense that they are discovering new knowledge and challenging existing interpretations (see Durnova, 2019). This sense, in turn, could elicit feelings of empowerment and motivate readers to continue reading the articles' propagandistic site, increasing their exposure to conservative rhetoric.

sections.paper_id: Karell and Agrawal - 2022 - Small town propaganda The content and emotions of politicized digital local news in the United Stat
paragraphs: How are politically conservative websites masquerading as local news websites attempt to broadcast their ideology and shape readers' understanding? To provide an initial answer to this question, we compared an original dataset of articles from conservative propagandistic websites to articles from websites of established, or institutional, local news organizations. The comparison comprised analyses of latent topics and expressed emotion. These two aspects can help covey ideology while obscuring its intent and enticing readers to engage the sites.

We found mostly minor differences in the content of propaganda and institutional local news media. However, the largest differences reveal two strategies for wrapping ideology in local news. First, propagandistic sites publish articles containing locally relevant text that is freely accessible online. Doing so makes the sites appear like legitimate local news sources and helps hide the ideological nature of rhetoric appearing in other articles on the site. Second, the propaganda has seized upon national political controversy more than institutional sites, likely seeing it as a vehicle for broadcasting ideology. In addition to these two strategies, an exploratory analysis found suggestive evidence of another strategy that can outlast political controversies. This third strategy entails publishing content about mundane economic issues with a subtle yet clearly conservative perspective on taxes, regulation, and 11 We assume propagandistic sites rely relatively heavily on scraping localized content from online sources because they lack the staff and local community connections that institutional local news organizations have. 12 The rolling median window is two sentences. Supplemental Material Appendix E presents a version of Figure 3 using the averaged rolling mean rather than median (Figure E1). business. It is potentially more unsettling than the relatively obvious rhetoric surrounding political events because this strategy uses less attention-grabbing content that is not tied to particular events. In other words, the locally focused propagandists can publish mundane yet politicized economic content every day, and likely without drawing condemnation. Furthermore, our results indicate that the relatively complex articles about local economic issues, along with its subtly embedded ideology, are difficult to detect using unsupervised machine learning methods. This kind of content was not modeled as its own topic, indicating that more sophisticated methods would be required for large-scale, automated identification.

We additionally found that the propagandistic sites use emotive words in particular ways. Our results indicate that the locally focused conservative propagandists publish local news stories offering readers a familiar feeling of their community (by mostly mirroring the content of corresponding legitimate digital news sites), albeit more often with slightly greater anger and disgust, while also at times using more anticipatory words at the end of articles. Such deployment of anticipatory words could generate in readers a sense of discovery in readers, thereby prompting further engagement with the propaganda. After all, the particular words and rhetoric in text can elicit emotional reactions in readers (Boeynaems, Burgers & Konijn, 2021).

There is much more to learn about propagandistic digital local news, and there are important reasons for doing so. This kind of propaganda, with its similarities to political native advertising, could damage local civic life by decreasing trust in legitimate digital local news sources whenever it is detected (Dai & Luqiu, 2020). Future research, whether it uses our data or not, can build on our findings in multiple ways. First, studies could examine the content and emotion of analogous sites published by politically left-leaning actors, perhaps in a comparative design using the sites in our database. Second, scholars could expand our database to include institutional articles that were published in sections that did not appear in the propaganda sites (e.g., Health, Arts, Books) and incorporate these data into a comparative analysis. Indeed, one limitation of our analysis is that we only compared institutional and propaganda articles labeled as being from the same sections. While this constraint on the data made for a more straightforward comparison, it could also have been partially responsible for the finding that there are only minor differences across media types.

A third possible extension is to examine how emotions vary across topics-anger could be more prevalent in political topics than in the gasoline price topic, for example-as well as to use analytical techniques that uncover patters in the text other than content and emotions, such as the use of metaphors and hyperbole (which can induce emotional reactions) (Boeynaems et al., 2021). This latter kind of work could additionally examine the ways that propagandists make their rhetoric fun (see Araujo, 2019). While most research focusing on the North American and European right-wing has highlighted its resentment, anger, and hatred, our analysis offers evidence that propagandists on the political right also makes its messaging exciting and empowering.

Future research could also link our database with other sources of data to study how localized conservative propaganda fits into the broader ecosystem of right-wing disinformation. This kind of work could explore the broader questions of, first, whether attention to political controversy is in fact used as a "smokescreen" for a duller yet nonetheless consequential economic agenda and, second, how propagandistic content and strategies circulate between local, national, and international outlets. Finally, we recommend that further research examines the offline social, economic, and political consequences of locally tailored propaganda, such as its potential to shape the local-level patterns in voter turnout, elections, laws, and civic engagement.
